data.title: Cooper Companies raises EPS and free cash flow guidance for 2026 amid robust MyDay and MiSight momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Cooper Companies (COO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Albert White reported "a strong start to the fiscal year, highlighted by product launches, outstanding profitability and robust cash flow." White emphasized progress on three strategic priorities: consistent market share gains for CooperVision, operational excellence translating to strong earnings and free cash flow, and disciplined capital allocation including $92 million in stock repurchases and debt reduction. He highlighted the global rollout of the MyDay daily silicone hydrogel portfolio and robust adoption of MiSight, particularly in EMEA and Japan.
  • White acknowledged regional dynamics: "The Americas and EMEA strengthened and have excellent commercial momentum. Japan weighed on our Asia Pac results, but we're executing on product launches and investing to restore growth in the region."
  • White stated, "Q1 earnings exceeded the top end of our guidance range, and those earnings translated into a healthy $159 million in free cash flow. Given our strong start to the year, we're raising guidance for both earnings and free cash flow."
  • On the strategic review announced in December, White said, "the review is progressing as planned with active engagement from our Board and advisers. We will communicate outcomes if we have something definitive to share or when the process is complete."
  • CFO Brian Andrews said, "Gross margin was 68.1%, exceeding expectations driven primarily by a lighter mix of low-margin Asia Pac revenue at CooperVision... Operating expenses rose only modestly and improved as a percentage of sales, declining from 43.6% to 41.2% year-over-year, reflecting the benefits of the reorganization executed in fiscal Q4 of last year."

Outlook

  • Andrews provided guidance for fiscal 2026: "consolidated revenues of roughly $4.3 billion to $4.35 billion, reflecting organic growth of roughly 4.5% to 5.5%. CooperVision revenue is expected to be in the range of $2.9 billion to $2.93 billion, up 4.5% to 5.5% organically. And CooperSurgical is expected to be in the range of $1.4 billion to $1.41 billion, up 4% to 5% organically."
  • Non-GAAP EPS guidance was raised to $4.58 to $4.66 "reflecting our Q1 beat and stronger expected operational performance."
  • Free cash flow guidance increased to $600 million to $625 million for 2026. For 2026 through 2028, the target remains "to generate more than $2.2 billion of free cash flow."
  • Guidance on tariffs, interest, and tax rates remain unchanged.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenues were $1.024 billion, up 6.2% or up 2.9% organically for Q1.
  • CooperVision revenue was $695 million, up 7.6% or up 3.3% organically. CooperSurgical revenue was $329 million, up 3.3% or up 2.2% organically.
  • Non-GAAP earnings grew 20% to $1.10. Free cash flow reached $159 million. The company repurchased 1.1 million shares for $92 million, made a final $50 million payment related to the Cook acquisition, and reduced net debt to $2.4 billion.
  • Operating margin improved to 26.9%, with operating income up 13.9%. Gross margin reached 68.1%.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Johnson, Robert W. Baird & Co.: Asked about reconciling the 10% calendar Q4 CVI growth versus recent quarters. White explained, "It's literally just a matter of months and shipment of product."
  • Johnson inquired about regaining above-market growth. White pointed to strong momentum in the Americas and EMEA, but noted Asia Pac, especially Japan, remains a challenge due to softness in older hydrogel products. He indicated, "Asia Pac will probably be down because of it. But then all of that success...happens in Q3, and you're going to have Asia Pac growing again."
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Probed about Middle East exposure. White responded, "the Middle East is about 2% of our sales...it could impact fertility because there's a decent amount of fertility business."
  • Biegelsen asked about margin phasing and tariffs. Andrews stated, "We've assumed $24 million in the year...a 10% tariff makes very little impact."
  • Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler: Inquired about new fit activity and industry price dynamics. White said, "we are taking wearers in both of those as we did this past quarter" and expressed confidence in positive pricing, though cautioned about Asia Pac competition.
  • Jonathan Block, Stifel: Asked about CVI organic growth underperformance and legal-related add backs. White attributed it to Japan's legacy products, while Andrews cited "$6.7 million was related to other legal-related matters."
  • Young Li, Jefferies: Sought updates on supply dynamics and Paragard. White confirmed supply constraints are resolved and contract wins are ongoing; Paragard was down 7% for the quarter.
  • Other analysts explored private label growth, MiSight market opportunity in Japan, the impact of Stellest, and R&D investments in myopia control.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but pressed on Asia Pac headwinds, CVI growth, and competitive threats, often probing for more detail on regional outlook and supply dynamics. Tone was neutral with pockets of skepticism on Japan and fertility.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone, repeatedly emphasizing execution, operational excellence, and visibility into growth drivers. Examples include White's "we are confident the region will return to growth in fiscal Q3" and Andrews' "I'm proud of the operational excellence we're seeing across the organization."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's sentiment shifted from cautious optimism to increased confidence, particularly around improved execution, cost controls, and the ability to deliver on raised guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for EPS and free cash flow was raised following a Q1 beat, compared to maintained guidance last quarter.
  • The strategic review remains ongoing, with no new details. Management continues to emphasize operational leverage and AI-driven efficiencies.
  • Analysts' focus on Asia Pac weakness, supply chain recovery, and competitive dynamics persisted, while management shifted to a more assertive outlook on margin improvement and regional recovery.
  • Revenue growth in CooperVision and CooperSurgical was slightly lower than in the previous quarter, and growth in Japan was again a notable drag.
  • Management confidence in overcoming Asia Pac challenges and driving consistent growth in Q3 and Q4 is more prominent this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued softness in Japan and legacy hydrogel products as a drag on Asia Pac, with recovery not expected until Q3.
  • Uncertainty in the Middle East, which represents 2% of sales, could impact fertility business.
  • Competitive pressures in Asia Pac and on Paragard were acknowledged, but no major updates on new entrants. White said, "We have not caved on price or anything along those lines."
  • Legal-related expenses were higher this quarter.
  • Management reiterated active engagement in the strategic review process but provided no timeline for completion.

Final Takeaway

The Cooper Companies entered fiscal 2026 with strong momentum, driven by robust performance in premium MyDay and MiSight portfolios, operational efficiencies, and disciplined capital allocation. Raised earnings and free cash flow guidance reflect confidence in the company’s ability to deliver sustained growth and improved profitability, supported by continued investment in innovation and expansion across key markets. Management remains focused on overcoming regional challenges, especially in Asia Pac, and executing on its strategic priorities to maximize long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump says U.S. must have a role in choosing Iran's next leader - Reuters
Teck Resources to sell zinc, silver, and germanium to Korea Zinc at higher prices in 2026
data.description:
• Canadian miner Teck Resources (TECK) has reportedly agreed with Korea Zinc Co. to sell its zinc concentrates at a slightly higher processing fee in 2026, while charging more on silver and germanium after a surge in prices for both metals.
  • The treatment charge that Korea Zinc will receive for smelting semi-processed ores known as concentrates rose to $85 a ton this year, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter.
  • Teck is one of the world's largest integrated germanium producers, a critical metal used in chipmaking. Zinc futures (LMZSDS03:COM) are up 4.3% so far this year, while spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) has advanced 18%.

data.title: Asian stocks trade mixed but face steep weekly losses as the Iran-Israel-US conflict hits its one-week mark
data.description:
Asian stocks turned mixed-to-higher on Friday, after a bruising week of geopolitical turmoil. Despite the intraday stabilization, the region remains on track for sharp weekly losses as escalating strife in the Middle East continues to fuel energy price volatility.

Meanwhile, the US-Israeli conflict with Iran entered its seventh day, with Iran launching missiles and drones across the Gulf and striking an oil refinery in Bahrain. While Israel continued airstrikes on Tehran, the US suspended operations at its embassy in Kuwait.

The Trump administration is weighing measures to counter surging oil and gasoline prices amid the war in Iran, Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said Thursday.

WTI crude futures fell more than 1% to around $79.6 per barrel on Friday, paring gains from the previous session.

Gold rose to around $5,130 per ounce on Friday but remained on track for its first weekly decline in five weeks.

South Korea's KOSPI index (KOSPI) fell more than 2% to around 5,450 on Friday, retreating after a sharp rebound in the previous session as rising oil prices and weaker global risk sentiment weighed on the market. The South Korean won strengthened to around 1,474 per dollar on Friday, gaining traction after stabilizing for two consecutive sessions as the government moved to reassure global investors.

Japan (NKY:IND) rose 0.48% to around 55,000, while the broader Topix Index slipped 0.4% to 3,688 on Friday, leaving both benchmarks on track to drop more than 6% for the week. The Japanese yen traded around 157.5 per dollar on Friday, on track for its third consecutive weekly decline.

Japan’s foreign reserves climbed $15.95 billion to $1.41 trillion in February 2026, marking the highest level since December 2021. 

China (SHCOMP) rose 0.28% to above 4,110, while the Shenzhen Component gained 0.4% to 14,140 on Friday, extending gains from the previous session as investors digested China’s latest economic outlook, and the offshore yuan rose to around 6.9 per dollar on Friday, recouping some of the previous session’s losses as market sentiment improved on China’s efforts to expand the currency’s role in international markets.

Furthermore, caution grew ahead of Chinese CPI and PPI data due next week, alongside combined January–February trade figures.

Hong Kong (HSI) rose 1.48% to 25,589 in Friday morning deals, building gains from the prior session.

India (SENSEX) fell 0.59% to 79,704 in morning trade on Friday, retreating from gains in the previous session as escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East lifted oil prices and dampened global risk appetite. The Indian rupee 

Australia (AS51) fell 1.00% to below 8,840 in early Friday trade, heading for a one-month low and nearly a 4% weekly loss, its biggest in a year. The Australian dollar traded around $0.703 on Friday, remaining volatile and on track for its first weekly decline since mid-January, as global risk sentiment worsened amid escalating Middle East tensions.

In the U.S. on Thursday, all three major indexes ended lower, extending their losses as the escalating conflict in Iran drove crude oil prices above $80 per barrel and sparked widespread fears of a global economic slowdown and resurging inflation.

U.S. stock futures edged higher on Friday following weakness on Wall Street overnight amid the risk of renewed inflation if energy prices remain elevated as the Middle East conflict persists: Dow +0.22%; S&P 500 +0.15%; Nasdaq +0.25%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Rohm surges after Denso reportedly makes ¥1.3T buyout offer
data.description:
• Rohm Co (ROHCF) (ROHCY) shares are set to surge by their daily limit in Tokyo trading on Friday after Nikkei reported Denso (DNZOF) has made a takeover approach valuing the chipmaker at around ¥1.3 trillion ($8.3 billion).
  • Rohm shares were untraded, but bid to their upper daily limit of ¥3,243.
  • The offer was reportedly made in February or earlier, Nikkei said. Rohm has since set up a special committee to consider whether to accept the proposal.
  • Denso is a key supplier to Toyota Motor (TM), while Rohm is known for its semiconductor and electronic component manufacturing.

data.title: Insurer Humana prices $1B junior subordinated notes offering due 2056
data.description:
• Humana (HUM) priced a public offering of $1 billion in aggregate principal amount of fixed-to-fixed rate junior subordinated notes due 2056 at 100.000 percent of the principal amount. The junior subordinated notes offering is expected to close on March 9, 2026.
  • The health insurance company expects net proceeds from the junior subordinated notes offering to be approximately $986 million and intends to use them for general corporate purposes, which may include the repayment of existing indebtedness, including borrowings under its commercial paper program.

data.title: Waste Connections prices $600M debt offering via issuance of senior notes
Baker Hughes prices $6.5B and €3B dual-currency debt offering
Trump says Iran wants deal; Tehran says it is not seeking ceasefire, warns U.S. invasion would be ‘big disaster’
data.description:
Iran’s foreign minister said Thursday that the country is “not asking for a ceasefire” with the United States and Israel and sees no reason to enter negotiations after nearly a week of fighting. Araghchi, in an exclusive interview with NBC News, said that Iran had not made a decision to start a war with its neighbors.

"We have not attacked our neighbors. We have not attacked Muslim countries," Araghchi said. "We have attacked American targets and American bases, American installations, which are unfortunately located in the soils of our neighbors."

"There is no way that they can win this war. As long as it takes, we resist," he said. "So they better stop this war and stop killing our people." He said it would be “a big disaster for them” if the U.S. military invaded Iran.

The comments come as President Donald Trump on Thursday said Tehran was reaching out to ​the United States about making a deal amid ‌U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, adding that further action to reduce pressure on oil was imminent.

"They're calling, they're saying 'how do we ​make a deal?' I said you're being ​a little bit late," said Trump, speaking at ⁠an event with the Inter Miami soccer team ​at the White House.

U.S. crude oil futures (CL1:COM) fell 1.2% to $80.04/bbl, and Brent crude (CO1:COM) was down about 1% to $84.59/bbl at press time.

data.title: Friday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Employment Situation

The most closely watched of all economic indicators, the employment situation is a set of monthly labor market indicators based on two separate reports: the establishment survey which tracks 650,000 worksites and offers the nonfarm payroll and average hourly earnings headlines and the household survey which interviews 60,000 households and generates the unemployment rate.

Jobs expected up 60K, a moderate showing these days, with the jobless rate up a tick at 4.4 percent versus 4.3 percent in January.

8:30 AM Retail Sales

Retail sales measure the total receipts at stores that sell merchandise and related services to final consumers.

Sales are expected to be basically flat, up 0.1 percent, ex-autos. Not impressive, especially as these are nominal numbers. 

10:00 AM Business Inventories 

Business inventories are the dollar amount of inventories held by manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers.

Inventories seen flat in December after edging up 0.1 percent in November.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

1:30 PM Beth Hammack Speaks

Cleveland Federal Reserve Bank President Beth Hammack participates in "On the Safe-haven Status of the Dollar" panel before the 2026 U.S. Monetary Policy Forum sponsored by the University of Chicago Booth School of Business.

3:00 PM Consumer Credit

The dollar value of consumer installment credit outstanding. Changes in consumer credit indicate the state of consumer finances and portend future spending patterns. 

A trend like a rise of $11.1 billion in the call after an unusually large $24 billion increase in the previous month.
